comment_text.sn22.57:1.3: The “seven” are the four noble truths from the previous discourse, plus the “gratification” triad from SN 22.26.
comment_text.sn22.57:18.1: The verb “examine” (upaparikkhati) is frequently used for someone who has learned the teachings, then internally reflects on their meaning.
comment_text.sn22.57:18.2: These three, along with the aggregates, are the major wisdom topics, mentioned together also at MN 115:3.3. More commonly, the aggregates, sense fields, and elements are mentioned as a summary of the Buddha’s wisdom teachings (SN 8.12:3.2, SN 35.31:1.21, Thig 5.8:2.2, etc.).

root_text.sn22.57:1.2: “Sattaṭṭhānakusalo, bhikkhave, bhikkhu tividhūpaparikkhī imasmiṁ dhammavinaye kevalī vusitavā uttamapurisoti vuccati.
root_text.sn22.57:1.4: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu rūpaṁ pajānāti, rūpasamudayaṁ pajānāti, rūpanirodhaṁ pajānāti, rūpanirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ pajānāti;
root_text.sn22.57:1.5: rūpassa assādaṁ pajānāti, rūpassa ādīnavaṁ pajānāti, rūpassa nissaraṇaṁ pajānāti;
root_text.sn22.57:1.9: viññāṇaṁ pajānāti, viññāṇasamudayaṁ pajānāti, viññāṇanirodhaṁ pajānāti, viññāṇanirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ pajānāti;
root_text.sn22.57:1.10: viññāṇassa assādaṁ pajānāti, viññāṇassa ādīnavaṁ pajānāti, viññāṇassa nissaraṇaṁ pajānāti.
root_text.sn22.57:2.2: Cattāro ca mahābhūtā, catunnañca mahābhūtānaṁ upādāya rūpaṁ.
root_text.sn22.57:2.6: Ayameva ariyo aṭṭhaṅgiko maggo rūpanirodhagāminī paṭipadā, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.sn22.57:3.1: Yaṁ rūpaṁ paṭicca uppajjati sukhaṁ somanassaṁ—
root_text.sn22.57:4.1: Ye hi keci, bhikkhave, samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā evaṁ rūpaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ rūpasamudayaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ rūpanirodhaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ rūpanirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ abhiññāya;
root_text.sn22.57:4.2: evaṁ rūpassa assādaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ rūpassa ādīnavaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ rūpassa nissaraṇaṁ abhiññāya rūpassa nibbidāya virāgāya nirodhāya paṭipannā, te suppaṭipannā.
root_text.sn22.57:4.3: Ye suppaṭipannā, te imasmiṁ dhammavinaye gādhanti.
root_text.sn22.57:5.1: Ye ca kho keci, bhikkhave, samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā evaṁ rūpaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ rūpasamudayaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ rūpanirodhaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ rūpanirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ abhiññāya;
root_text.sn22.57:5.2: evaṁ rūpassa assādaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ rūpassa ādīnavaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ rūpassa nissaraṇaṁ abhiññāya rūpassa nibbidā virāgā nirodhā anupādā vimuttā, te suvimuttā.
root_text.sn22.57:5.4: Ye kevalino vaṭṭaṁ tesaṁ natthi paññāpanāya.
root_text.sn22.57:6.8: Ayameva ariyo aṭṭhaṅgiko maggo vedanānirodhagāminī paṭipadā, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.sn22.57:7.1: Yaṁ vedanaṁ paṭicca uppajjati sukhaṁ somanassaṁ—
root_text.sn22.57:8.1: Ye hi, keci, bhikkhave, samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā evaṁ vedanaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ vedanāsamudayaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ vedanānirodhaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ vedanānirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ abhiññāya;
root_text.sn22.57:8.2: evaṁ vedanāya assādaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ vedanāya ādīnavaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ vedanāya nissaraṇaṁ abhiññāya vedanāya nibbidāya virāgāya nirodhāya paṭipannā, te suppaṭipannā.
root_text.sn22.57:8.3: Ye suppaṭipannā, te imasmiṁ dhammavinaye gādhanti.
root_text.sn22.57:9.1: Ye ca kho keci, bhikkhave, samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā evaṁ vedanaṁ abhiññāya …pe…
root_text.sn22.57:10.3: rūpasaññā, saddasaññā, gandhasaññā, rasasaññā, phoṭṭhabbasaññā, dhammasaññā.
root_text.sn22.57:10.7: Ayameva ariyo aṭṭhaṅgiko maggo saññānirodhagāminī paṭipadā, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.sn22.57:11.8: Ayameva ariyo aṭṭhaṅgiko maggo saṅkhāranirodhagāminī paṭipadā, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.sn22.57:12.1: Yaṁ saṅkhāre paṭicca uppajjati sukhaṁ somanassaṁ—
root_text.sn22.57:13.1: Ye hi keci, bhikkhave, samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā evaṁ saṅkhāre abhiññāya, evaṁ saṅkhārasamudayaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ saṅkhāranirodhaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ saṅkhāranirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ abhiññāya …pe… saṅkhārānaṁ nibbidāya virāgāya nirodhāya paṭipannā te suppaṭipannā.
root_text.sn22.57:13.2: Ye suppaṭipannā, te imasmiṁ dhammavinaye gādhanti …pe…
root_text.sn22.57:14.3: cakkhuviññāṇaṁ, sotaviññāṇaṁ, ghānaviññāṇaṁ, jivhāviññāṇaṁ, kāyaviññāṇaṁ, manoviññāṇaṁ.
root_text.sn22.57:14.7: Ayameva ariyo aṭṭhaṅgiko maggo viññāṇanirodhagāminī paṭipadā, seyyathidaṁ—
root_text.sn22.57:15.1: Yaṁ viññāṇaṁ paṭicca uppajjati sukhaṁ somanassaṁ—
root_text.sn22.57:16.1: Ye hi keci, bhikkhave, samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā evaṁ viññāṇaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ viññāṇasamudayaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ viññāṇanirodhaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ viññāṇanirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ abhiññāya;
root_text.sn22.57:16.2: evaṁ viññāṇassa assādaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ viññāṇassa ādīnavaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ viññāṇassa nissaraṇaṁ abhiññāya viññāṇassa nibbidāya virāgāya nirodhāya paṭipannā, te suppaṭipannā.
root_text.sn22.57:16.3: Ye suppaṭipannā, te imasmiṁ dhammavinaye gādhanti.
root_text.sn22.57:17.1: Ye ca kho keci, bhikkhave, samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā evaṁ viññāṇaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ viññāṇasamudayaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ viññāṇanirodhaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ viññāṇanirodhagāminiṁ paṭipadaṁ abhiññāya;
root_text.sn22.57:17.2: evaṁ viññāṇassa assādaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ viññāṇassa ādīnavaṁ abhiññāya, evaṁ viññāṇassa nissaraṇaṁ abhiññāya viññāṇassa nibbidā virāgā nirodhā anupādā vimuttā, te suvimuttā.
root_text.sn22.57:17.4: Ye kevalino vaṭṭaṁ tesaṁ natthi paññāpanāya.
root_text.sn22.57:17.5: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu sattaṭṭhānakusalo hoti.
root_text.sn22.57:18.2: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu dhātuso upaparikkhati, āyatanaso upaparikkhati, paṭiccasamuppādaso upaparikkhati.
root_text.sn22.57:18.3: Evaṁ kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu tividhūpaparikkhī hoti.
root_text.sn22.57:18.4: Sattaṭṭhānakusalo, bhikkhave, bhikkhu tividhūpaparikkhī, imasmiṁ dhammavinaye kevalī vusitavā ‘uttamapuriso’ti vuccatī”ti.

translation_text.sn22.57:1.2: “Mendicants, in this teaching and training a mendicant who is skilled in seven cases and who examines in three ways is called consummate, accomplished, a supreme person.
translation_text.sn22.57:1.3: And how is a mendicant skilled in seven cases?
translation_text.sn22.57:1.4: It’s when a mendicant understands form, its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation.
translation_text.sn22.57:1.5: They understand form’s gratification, drawback, and escape.
translation_text.sn22.57:1.9: consciousness, its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation.
translation_text.sn22.57:1.10: They understand consciousness’s gratification, drawback, and escape.
translation_text.sn22.57:2.2: The four principal states, and form derived from the four principal states.
translation_text.sn22.57:2.6: The practice that leads to the cessation of form is simply this noble eightfold path, that is:
translation_text.sn22.57:2.7: right view, right thought, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.
translation_text.sn22.57:3.1: The pleasure and happiness that arise from form: this is its gratification.
translation_text.sn22.57:3.3: That form is impermanent, suffering, and perishable: this is its drawback.
translation_text.sn22.57:3.5: Removing and giving up desire and greed for form: this is its escape.
translation_text.sn22.57:4.1: Those ascetics and brahmins who have directly known form in this way—and its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation;
translation_text.sn22.57:4.2: its gratification, drawback, and escape—and are practicing for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding form: they are practicing well.
translation_text.sn22.57:4.3: Those who practice well have a footing in this teaching and training.
translation_text.sn22.57:5.1: Those ascetics and brahmins who have directly known form in this way—and its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation;
translation_text.sn22.57:5.2: its gratification, drawback, and escape—and due to disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding form, are freed by not grasping: they are well freed.
translation_text.sn22.57:5.3: Those who are well freed are consummate ones.
translation_text.sn22.57:5.4: For consummate ones, there is no cycle of rebirths to be found.
translation_text.sn22.57:6.2: There are these six classes of feeling:
translation_text.sn22.57:6.8: The practice that leads to the cessation of feelings is simply this noble eightfold path, that is:
translation_text.sn22.57:6.9: right view, right thought, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.
translation_text.sn22.57:7.1: The pleasure and happiness that arise from feeling: this is its gratification.
translation_text.sn22.57:7.3: That feeling is impermanent, suffering, and perishable: this is its drawback.
translation_text.sn22.57:7.5: Removing and giving up desire and greed for feeling: this is its escape. …
translation_text.sn22.57:10.2: There are these six classes of perception:
translation_text.sn22.57:10.3: perceptions of sights, sounds, smells, tastes, touches, and ideas.
translation_text.sn22.57:10.7: The practice that leads to the cessation of perceptions is simply this noble eightfold path …
translation_text.sn22.57:11.2: There are these six classes of intention:
translation_text.sn22.57:11.8: The practice that leads to the cessation of choices is simply this noble eightfold path …
translation_text.sn22.57:14.2: There are these six classes of consciousness:
translation_text.sn22.57:14.3: eye, ear, nose, tongue, body, and mind consciousness.
translation_text.sn22.57:14.5: Consciousness originates from name and form.
translation_text.sn22.57:14.6: When name and form cease, consciousness ceases.
translation_text.sn22.57:14.7: The practice that leads to the cessation of consciousness is simply this noble eightfold path, that is:
translation_text.sn22.57:14.8: right view, right thought, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right immersion.
translation_text.sn22.57:15.1: The pleasure and happiness that arise from consciousness: this is its gratification.
translation_text.sn22.57:15.3: That consciousness is impermanent, suffering, and perishable: this is its drawback.
translation_text.sn22.57:15.5: Removing and giving up desire and greed for consciousness: this is its escape.
translation_text.sn22.57:16.1: Those ascetics and brahmins who have directly known consciousness in this way—and its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation;
translation_text.sn22.57:16.2: its gratification, drawback, and escape—and are practicing for disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding consciousness: they are practicing well.
translation_text.sn22.57:16.3: Those who practice well have a footing  in this teaching and training.
translation_text.sn22.57:17.1: Those ascetics and brahmins who have directly known consciousness in this way—and its origin, its cessation, and the practice that leads to its cessation;
translation_text.sn22.57:17.2: its gratification, drawback, and escape—and due to disillusionment, dispassion, and cessation regarding consciousness, are freed by not grasping: they are well freed.
translation_text.sn22.57:17.3: Those who are well freed are consummate ones.
translation_text.sn22.57:17.4: For consummate ones, there is no cycle of rebirths to be found.
translation_text.sn22.57:17.5: That’s how a mendicant is skilled in seven cases.
translation_text.sn22.57:18.1: And how does a mendicant examine in three ways?
translation_text.sn22.57:18.2: It’s when a mendicant examines by way of the elements, sense fields, and dependent origination.
translation_text.sn22.57:18.3: That’s how a mendicant examines in three ways.
translation_text.sn22.57:18.4: In this teaching and training, a mendicant who is skilled in seven cases and who examines in three ways is called consummate, accomplished, a supreme person.”

reference_text.sn22.57:1.1: csp1ed13.51, csp2ed13.51, dr17.75, ms13S3_272, msdiv57, pts-vp-pli3.62, vri25.57
